title: Watercolour backgrounds for journal pages
subtitle: Laying flat washes, gradient washes, and wet-on-wet blooms as journal page backgrounds
excerpt: A watercolour wash applied before writing and decoration gives a journal page warmth, structure, and a sense of place. Three washes are covered: a flat single-colour wash across the whole page, a two-colour gradient, and a wet-on-wet bloom where colours bloom unpredictably into each other.
sourceNotes: Watercolour wash techniques are foundational to traditional watercolour painting. The application described here is adapted for journal pages in mixed-media sketchbooks. Standard watercolour wash methods are documented in numerous pre-copyright instructional materials including John Ruskin, Elements of Drawing (1857).

glossaryTerms.definition: A uniform layer of diluted watercolour applied across a surface in overlapping horizontal strokes, producing a single even colour.
A watercolour wash that transitions smoothly from a strong colour at one edge to pale or clear at the other, produced by progressively diluting the paint or by tilting the page.
A watercolour technique in which pigment is applied to a wet paper surface, causing the paint to spread and bloom unpredictably into the surrounding wet area.

content.text: A watercolour background works best when applied before any writing or sticking. The water in the wash buckles the page slightly; allowing it to dry fully and flatten before writing prevents pen and pencil marks from dragging. The three techniques described here range from controlled to deliberately uncontrolled: the
gives an even colour field, the
creates a horizon or fade, and the
bloom lets the colours find their own edges.

items.name: Watercolour paints (pan or tube, 2 to 3 colours)
Journal with paper at least 120 gsm (lighter paper will buckle excessively)
Masking tape (to tape the journal open flat while washing, if the pages curl)

content.text: Mix a generous pool of diluted colour in the palette. The mix should be more water than paint; a mid-tone is more readable behind writing than a dark value.

Load the brush fully and draw a horizontal stroke across the top of the page, leaving a small bead of wet paint at the lower edge of the stroke. Immediately draw the next stroke slightly overlapping the bead, picking it up and pulling it across. Continue to the bottom of the page. Each stroke picks up the bead from the previous one, producing an even colour without hard lines.
Blot any pooled paint at the bottom edge with the corner of a dry tissue. Let dry flat.

Start the same as the flat wash, but dilute the paint slightly more with each successive stroke as you work down the page. By the bottom third, the brush should carry almost clear water. Alternatively, apply a normal flat wash and then, while still wet, tilt the journal so the paint flows toward the bottom edge, deepening the lower third.
Wet the whole page with clean water using a wide flat brush or the fully loaded round brush. The paper should shine with moisture but have no standing puddles.

Drop concentrated paint from the brush tip onto the wet surface in different areas of the page. The paint will bloom outward in soft-edged clouds. Drop a second colour nearby and let it bloom into the first.
Do not touch the blooms once applied. Any brush movement while the paper is wet smears the soft edges. Set the journal flat and let dry completely before writing on the page.
